document: Financial Services and Markets Act 2023 (FSMA 2023) (United Kingdom - Row 31)

sections.heading: Revocation of [F2assimilated] law relating to financial services and markets
sections.title: Revocation of financial services legislation
sections.summary: This section revokes specified legislation related to financial services and markets. It defines which types of legislation are affected by revocation, including primary legislation, subordinate legislation, EU tertiary legislation, and other EU-derived legislation. The revocation does not affect amendments made to the revoked legislation prior to revocation, ensuring continuity of those amendments. The Treasury may issue regulations to exclude certain subordinate legislation from Part 5 of Schedule 1, subject to negative procedure.

requirements.text: The legislation referred to in Schedule 1 is revoked
Part 1 refers to [F3assimilated direct principal] legislation
Part 2 refers to subordinate legislation
Part 3 refers to EU tertiary legislation and subordinate legislation made under an instrument referred to in Part 2
Part 4 refers to primary legislation
Part 5 refers to other EU-derived legislation not covered by Parts 1 to 3

The revocation of any legislation in accordance with this section does not affect the continued effect of any amendments to other legislation made by that revoked legislation (as those amendments had effect immediately before the revocation)

The Treasury may by regulations provide for specified subordinate legislation, or for subordinate legislation of a specified description, otherwise falling within Part 5 of Schedule 1, not to fall within that Part
Regulations under subsection (5) are subject to the negative procedure

sections.source_quote: Revocation of [F2assimilated] law relating to financial services and markets (1) The legislation referred to in Schedule 1 is revoked.

sections.heading: Transitional amendments
sections.title: Transitional amendments for revoked legislation
sections.summary: This section allows Schedule 2 to amend particular legislation during the transitional period following revocation. The transitional period ends with the revocation of the legislation. Amendments in Schedule 2 do not restrict the Treasury's power to modify legislation as amended by Schedule 2. The section specifies when the transitional provisions come into force for different purposes.

requirements.text: Schedule 2 amends particular legislation referred to in Schedule 1 in relation to the transitional period

Amendments in Schedule 2 do not restrict the Treasury's power to modify legislation as amended by Schedule 2

sections.source_quote: 2 Schedule 2 amends particular legislation referred to in Schedule 1 in relation to the transitional period.

sections.heading: Power to make further transitional amendments
sections.title: Power to modify saved legislation
sections.summary: This section grants the Treasury power to modify legislation saved by revocation. Modifications can be made for clarity or accessibility, or for purposes specified in section 3(2). The Treasury may make further modifications to restated legislation. The power does not include modifying primary legislation or technical standards. Regulations modifying EU tertiary legislation or subordinate legislation not subject to affirmative resolution are subject to negative procedure.

requirements.text: The power under section 86(5) to make saving provision in connection with the revocation of any legislation referred to in Schedule 1 includes power to restate that legislation (as it has effect immediately before its revocation)

sections.heading: Power to restate and modify saved legislation
sections.title: Power to replace references to EU directives
sections.summary: This section allows the Treasury to modify legislation to replace references to EU directives with other provisions. The power is exercisable only for clarity or accessibility, or for purposes specified in section 3(2). Regulations amending primary legislation are subject to affirmative procedure.

requirements.text: The Treasury may by regulations modify legislation for or in connection with the purpose of replacing any reference (however expressed) to an EU directive referred to in Part 3 of Schedule 1 with such other provision (if any) as the Treasury consider appropriate

The power under subsection (1) is exercisable only if the Treasury consider it necessary or desirable to replace the reference for or in connection with the purpose of making the law clearer or more accessible

sections.title: Exemptions from consultation requirements for rules
sections.summary: This section exempts regulators from certain consultation requirements when making or revoking rules that reduce regulatory burden without material effects. Rules that make excluded provision in legislation or excluded changes to existing rules are exempt from relevant requirements. Regulators must publish statements explaining the reasons for reducing regulatory burden when exempting themselves from consultation requirements.

requirements.text: A relevant requirement does not apply to the making of rules by a regulator if and to the extent that the proposed rules make excluded provision in relation to provisions of legislation referred to in Schedule 1

A relevant requirement does not apply to the making of rules by a regulator if and to the extent that the proposed rules make excluded changes to provision of existing rules made by the regulator containing [F6an assimilated] obligation
A relevant requirement does not apply to the revocation of rules by a regulator if and to the extent that the rules being revoked make provision containing [F8an assimilated] obligation
Rules make excluded provision in relation to provisions of legislation if, in the opinion of the regulator making the rules, the rules reproduce those provisions without any changes that are material

Rules make excluded provision in relation to provisions of legislation if, in the opinion of the regulator making the rules, the rules reproduce those provisions with changes that are material but their effect is to reduce a regulatory burden without having any other effects that are material
Rules make excluded changes to provision of existing rules if, in the opinion of the regulator making the rules, the effect of the changes is to reduce a regulatory burden
Rules make excluded changes to provision of existing rules if, in the opinion of the regulator making the rules, the changes have no other effects that are material
In this section references to a 'regulatory burden' include references to a financial cost
In this section references to a 'regulatory burden' include references to an administrative inconvenience
In this section references to a 'regulatory burden' include references to an obstacle to trade or innovation

Where a relevant requirement does not apply to the making or revocation of rules by virtue of subsection (1), (2) or (3), the requirement also does not apply to any rules that contain incidental, supplemental, consequential or transitional provision so far as made in connection with provision made by virtue of that subsection

Where a regulator makes or revokes rules without complying with a relevant requirement by virtue of subsection (1), (2) or (3), the regulator must publish a statement which must list the provisions of legislation that have been restated by the rules

Where a regulator makes or revokes rules without complying with a relevant requirement by virtue of subsection (1), (2) or (3), the regulator must publish a statement which must specify or describe [F9the assimilated] obligations in relation to which changes have been made by the rules

Where a regulator makes or revokes rules without complying with a relevant requirement by virtue of subsection (1), (2) or (3), the regulator must publish a statement which must specify or describe [F10the assimilated] obligations that have been removed by the revocation of the rules

sections.title: Rules for central counterparties and depositories
sections.summary: This section outlines rules for central counterparties and central securities depositories, including the Bank of England's authority to make rules for FMI entities to ensure financial stability. It specifies that rules for overseas FMI entities must align with corresponding UK rules and be authorized by Treasury regulations, with systemic third country CCPs exempt from certain restrictions.
sections.dtia_summary: Regulates cross-border financial infrastructure, requiring alignment of rules for overseas central counterparties with UK standards, impacting data transfers related to financial market infrastructure.

requirements.text: The Bank of England may make rules applying to FMI entities for financial stability
FMI entities include recognised central counterparties, recognised CSDs, third country central counterparties, and third country CSDs
Rules for third country central counterparties must be subject to Treasury regulations
Systemic third country CCPs are exempt from the restrictions on overseas FMI entities

sections.source_quote: The Bank of England may make such rules applying to FMI entities—(a) with respect to the carrying on by them of relevant regulated activities, or (b) with respect to the carrying on by them of an activity which is not a relevant regulated activity, as appear to the Bank to be necessary or expedient for the purpose of advancing its Financial Stability Objective.

sections.heading: F10 (cont.)
sections.title: Designated activities regime for financial services
sections.summary: This section establishes a designated activities regime where the Treasury may regulate specific financial activities as 'designated activities' to ensure compliance with financial stability objectives. Designated activities must relate to UK financial markets, financial instruments, or financial investments. The FCA may impose rules and directions on designated activities, including restrictions on carrying out activities outside the UK and requiring compliance with specific conditions.
sections.dtia_summary: Establishes a framework for regulating cross-border financial activities, requiring designated activities to comply with UK regulations even when conducted outside the UK, impacting data transfers related to financial services.

requirements.text: The Treasury may by regulations provide for an activity to be a designated activity

The FCA may by directions impose requirements on persons carrying on designated activities

sections.source_quote: The Treasury may by regulations provide for an activity of a specified description to be a designated activity for the purposes of this Act.

sections.heading: Central counterparties and central securities depositories: other requirements
sections.title: Bank imposes requirements on central counterparties and CSDs
sections.summary: The Bank of England may impose requirements on recognised central counterparties, central securities depositories, and systemic third country CCPs if the Bank determines it is desirable for financial stability or the entity has failed to meet recognition or compliance obligations. The power cannot restrict discretionary payments to employees or shareholders of central counterparties. The Bank's requirements follow the FCA's framework with modifications to reference the Bank instead of the FCA.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes conditions under which the Bank of England can impose requirements on central counterparties and CSDs, affecting data transfer and processing across borders through financial stability safeguards and compliance checks.

requirements.text: The Bank may impose requirements on a recognised central counterparty, recognised CSD, or systemic third country CCP

sections.heading: Rules relating to investment exchanges and data reporting service providers
sections.title: FCA rules for investment exchanges and data reporting services
sections.summary: The FCA may make rules for recognised UK investment exchanges or data reporting service providers to advance operational objectives, including activities related to regulated activities or data reporting services. Rules may apply even without direct relationships to protected persons and cannot modify retained EU legislation.
sections.dtia_summary: This section allows the FCA to create rules for investment exchanges and data reporting services that impact cross-border data flows, with specific conditions on applicability and EU legislation compatibility.

sections.summary: This section defines 'data reporting service' and 'data reporting service provider' as per the Data Reporting Services Regulations 2017, and clarifies 'recognised UK investment exchange' as a non-overseas exchange. It also updates references in other sections to include data reporting service providers.

sections.title: Regulatory principles including competitiveness and growth objective
sections.summary: This section adds the competitiveness and growth objective to regulatory principles. It requires regulators to contribute to achieving compliance with the UK's net zero emissions target and environmental targets where relevant. The objective is defined as facilitating international competitiveness and long-term growth of the UK economy, including the financial services sector.
sections.dtia_summary: Regulators must align their operations with the competitiveness and growth objective, which may affect data transfers by requiring compliance with international standards for financial services.

requirements.text: Regulators must contribute to achieving compliance with the UK net zero emissions target and environmental targets
Competitiveness and growth objective facilitates international competitiveness of the UK economy
Competitiveness and growth objective facilitates long-term growth of the UK economy

sections.source_quote: The competitiveness and growth objective is: facilitating, subject to aligning with relevant international standards—(a) the international competitiveness of the economy of the United Kingdom (including in particular the financial services sector), and (b) its growth in the medium to long term.

sections.title: Matters to consider when making rules
sections.summary: This section requires regulators to consider specified matters when making rules. The specified matters may be relevant to the rules' purpose, including the power under which rules are made, persons to whom rules apply, and activities to which rules relate. Regulators must explain how specified matters have affected proposed rules, with exceptions for material changes or delays that would be prejudicial to consumers or policyholders.
sections.dtia_summary: Regulators must consider specified matters when making rules, which may impact data transfers by requiring alignment with international standards for financial services.

sections.title: Critical third parties: related amendments
sections.summary: This section amends multiple provisions of the Financial Services and Markets Act 2023 to define 'critical third party' and update references to critical third parties in various contexts, including injunctions, publication, third party rights, and rules made by the Bank. It specifies that critical third parties are defined in Chapter 3C and modifies several sections to include critical third parties in relevant lists and requirements.
sections.dtia_summary: Critical third parties are defined and integrated into regulatory frameworks for financial institutions, requiring specific safeguards and oversight in cross-border data transfers and payment systems involving critical third parties.

sections.title: Financial promotion approval requirements
sections.summary: This section introduces requirements for financial promotion approvals, mandating that an authorized person must obtain permission from the FCA before approving communication content for financial promotion purposes. It outlines the conditions for granting permission, including the need for FCA consultation with the PRA and Gibraltar regulator, and specifies that approvals must align with the FCA's operational objectives.
sections.dtia_summary: Financial promotion approvals require FCA oversight, ensuring that communications meet regulatory standards before being disseminated, which impacts data transfer and processing for financial promotions.

sections.source_quote: An authorized person must not approve the content of a communication for the purposes of section 21 unless the person has permission to do so given by the FCA under this section.

sections.heading: I68 (cont.)
sections.title: Implementation of mutual recognition agreements
sections.summary: This section allows the Treasury to make regulations for implementing mutual recognition agreements with foreign countries. It defines relevant matters as those related to financial services or markets. The regulations can confer powers on the Treasury or relevant regulators, impose duties on regulators, address fees, enforcement, appeals, and information sharing. The Treasury must consult regulators before imposing duties on them. Regulations are subject to the affirmative procedure.
sections.dtia_summary: This section enables the UK to implement mutual recognition agreements with foreign countries for financial services, which may affect data transfers by allowing equivalent legal frameworks across borders. It requires regulators to maintain specific reporting on compliance with competitiveness and growth objectives.

sections.heading: I89 (cont.)
sections.title: Regulator consultation on actions affecting international deference decisions
sections.summary: Regulators must assess the impact of proposed actions on notified deference decisions. If a regulator identifies material risk of incompatibility with a notified deference decision, it must consult the Treasury. Relevant actions include rule-making or policy changes affecting supervisory functions. The duty to consult applies to certain actions but excludes specific cases like product intervention rules under section 137D where consumer protection or integrity objectives are advanced.
sections.dtia_summary: Regulators must ensure proposed actions do not undermine international legal equivalence decisions before implementation, requiring consultation with Treasury to maintain cross-border regulatory alignment.

sections.title: Regulator responses to Treasury recommendations
sections.summary: The FCA and PRA must respond to Treasury recommendations within 12 months by notifying written actions taken or intended, or reasons for non-compliance. They must update the Treasury annually on progress unless explicitly exempted. This ensures transparency and accountability in implementing Treasury guidance.
sections.dtia_summary: Regulators must provide timely updates to the Treasury on compliance with recommendations, ensuring alignment between domestic regulatory actions and international fiscal policy objectives.

sections.title: FCA and PRA statutory panels: appointment and oversight
sections.summary: This section mandates the FCA and PRA to establish and maintain statutory panels representing relevant stakeholders. The FCA must appoint members to represent issuers and investors, while the PRA must appoint representatives for insurance practitioners. Both regulators require Treasury approval for chair appointments and must ensure panels include individuals with specific expertise and employment backgrounds. The panels operate under the oversight of section 1MA.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes statutory panels for FCA and PRA to ensure stakeholder representation in regulatory decision-making. It requires specific composition and oversight mechanisms that may impact data sharing and processing across borders when regulators engage with external bodies.

sections.title: FCA and PRA panel appointment statements
sections.summary: The FCA and PRA must publish statements detailing their panel appointment processes. These statements can include additional information the regulator considers appropriate. Regulators may update or replace statements but must consult the Treasury before publication. Statements must be published in a manner that effectively reaches the public. The term 'statutory panel' refers to specific panels established under designated sections.
sections.dtia_summary: Regulators must publish statements on panel appointments with Treasury consultation requirements. This affects transparency and accountability in panel selection processes but has limited direct impact on cross-border data transfers.

sections.heading: "(fa) (cont.)
sections.title: Bank of England regulatory powers for financial market infrastructure
sections.summary: The Bank of England must exercise its functions related to central counterparties and central securities depositaries while considering financial stability objectives. The Bank must follow regulatory principles that prioritize economic efficiency, proportionality, sustainable growth, consumer responsibility, and transparency. The Bank's FMI functions include rule-making, technical standards, and code preparation under FSMA 2000.
sections.dtia_summary: The Bank's FMI functions require adherence to regulatory principles that impact financial stability and innovation. This section has indirect relevance to data transfer mechanisms through the Bank's oversight of financial infrastructure.

requirements.text: The Bank must have regard to regulatory principles in section 30E when exercising FMI functions.
The Bank must have regard to the effects on financial stability of countries or territories outside the UK when exercising FMI functions.
The Bank must have regard to the desirability of exercising FMI functions regardless of the location of persons receiving FMI services.
The Bank must act to facilitate innovation in FMI services with a view to improving service quality, efficiency, and economy.
The Bank's FMI functions include making rules under FSMA 2000, making technical standards, preparing and issuing codes, and determining general policy and principles.
FMI entities include recognised central counterparties, recognised CSDs, third country central counterparties, and third country CSDs.
FMI services are services provided by FMI entities as part of their business.

sections.source_quote: The Bank must have regard to the regulatory principles in section 30E when exercising FMI functions in a way that advances the Financial Stability Objective (and subject to that).

sections.heading: "(fa) (cont.)
sections.title: Regulatory amendments to financial stability objectives
sections.summary: This section amends the Financial Services and Markets Act 2000 by inserting specific provisions into the Bank of England's regulatory framework. It modifies the definition of 'FMI functions' to include the Bank of England's role in financial stability, and adjusts the application of certain sections to ensure alignment with the Bank's Financial Stability Objective and other regulatory objectives under the Bank of England Act 1998.
sections.dtia_summary: This section requires that the Bank of England's FMI functions align with financial stability objectives, impacting how data transfers related to financial stability are assessed.

sections.summary: This section amends the Financial Services and Markets Act 2000 to integrate the Payment Systems Regulator into the FCA Board structure. sections.source_quote: The Chair of the Payment Systems Regulator must not take part in any discussion by or decision of the FCA which relates to the exercise of the FCA's functions in relation to a particular person.

sections.heading: Control over authorised persons
sections.title: Regulatory amendments to financial stability objectives
sections.summary: This section amends the Financial Services and Markets Act 2000 by modifying the approval conditions for authorized persons. It requires that any conditions imposed by regulators must be in line with the regulator's objectives to advance financial stability, subject to specific exceptions.
sections.dtia_summary: This section impacts data transfer assessments by requiring that regulatory conditions for authorized persons align with financial stability objectives.

sections.summary: This section amends the Financial Services and Markets Act 2000 to update the financial services compensation scheme. sections.dtia_summary: This section affects data transfer assessments by modifying the financial services compensation scheme to remove outdated provisions.

sections.heading: The Ombudsman scheme
sections.title: Ombudsman scheme amendments
sections.summary: This section amends the Ombudsman scheme by allowing the Treasury to specify descriptions of persons for the scheme, with the requirement that the Treasury must consult the scheme operator before making such regulations. It also clarifies that eligible complainants are those who fall within the compulsory or voluntary jurisdiction of the ombudsman scheme.
sections.dtia_summary: This section impacts data transfer assessments by allowing the Treasury to specify descriptions of persons for the ombudsman scheme, with consultation requirements.

Before making regulations under sub-paragraph (3) the Treasury must consult the scheme operator.

sections.source_quote: Before making regulations under sub-paragraph (3) the Treasury must consult the scheme operator.

sections.heading: Unauthorised co-ownership AIFs
sections.title: Unauthorised co-ownership AIFs: regulatory framework
sections.summary: This section modifies section 261E to insert a definition of unauthorised co-ownership AIFs and adjusts contractual language. It establishes that unauthorised co-ownership AIFs are AIFs not authorised under an authorisation order. The Treasury may make regulations corresponding to sections 261M-261O and 261P(1) for unauthorised co-ownership AIFs.

sections.heading: Power to amend enactments in consequence of rules
sections.title: Power to amend consequential enactments via regulations
sections.summary: This section allows the Treasury to make regulations amending enactments that are consequential on rules. It defines 'enactment' to include subordinate legislation, retained EU legislation, and enactments from devolved legislatures. The Treasury may also amend other provisions of FSMA 2000 through regulations, subject to affirmative procedure.

sections.heading: Power to amend or repeal certain provisions of FSMA 2000
sections.title: Power to amend FSMA 2000 provisions via regulations
sections.summary: This section grants the Treasury the power to amend or repeal specific provisions in Part 9C of FSMA 2000, including sections 143C, 143D, and 143G. It also allows the Treasury to amend other provisions of FSMA 2000 through regulations, subject to the affirmative procedure.

sections.heading: Cryptoassets
sections.title: Cryptoasset definitions and regulatory scope
sections.summary: This section adds 'cryptoasset' to the definitions of financial promotion and regulated activities, providing a specific definition of cryptoassets as cryptographically secured digital representations of value or contractual rights that can be transferred electronically and use distributed ledger technology. It also allows the Treasury to amend the cryptoasset definition via regulations.

requirements.text: Cryptoasset means any cryptographically secured digital representation of value or contractual rights that can be transferred, stored or traded electronically and uses technology supporting the recording or storage of data (which may include distributed ledger technology).

sections.source_quote: Cryptoasset means any cryptographically secured digital representation of value or contractual rights that can be transferred, stored or traded electronically and uses technology supporting the recording or storage of data (which may include distributed ledger technology).

sections.heading: Bank of England levy
sections.title: Bank of England levy on financial institutions
sections.summary: Section 70 amends the Bank of England Act 1998 to introduce a levy on eligible financial institutions. It defines eligible institutions as authorized deposit-takers excluding credit unions, friendly societies, and certain insurance entities. The levy is determined annually by the Bank based on policy functions and previous year's requirements, with payment obligations specified for institutions. The levy is recoverable as a civil debt with interest payable at 4% above the benchmark rate.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes a financial levy mechanism that impacts cross-border data flows through financial institutions, requiring specific payment obligations and interest calculations for unpaid levies.

requirements.text: The Bank may impose a charge on eligible institutions in accordance with this Schedule
An 'eligible institution' is a person who, at any time during a levy year, is an authorised deposit-taker
An 'authorised deposit-taker' for these purposes is a person who has permission under Part 4A of the Financial Services and Markets Act 2000 to accept deposits
The Bank must determine which policy functions it intends to fund by means of the levy
The Bank must determine the total amount of the levy it reasonably considers it requires in connection with the funding of those functions

Interest is payable at the rate equivalent to an annual percentage rate of 4% above the benchmark rate on unpaid amounts

sections.source_quote: The Bank may impose a charge on eligible institutions in accordance with this Schedule. An 'eligible institution' is a person who, at any time during a levy year, is an authorised deposit-taker.

sections.heading: Politically exposed persons: money laundering and terrorist financing
sections.title: Politically exposed persons: risk assessment and enhanced due diligence
sections.summary: This section requires the Treasury to make regulations that adjust customer due diligence for domestic politically exposed persons (PEPs) and their family members or close associates. It specifies that domestic PEPs present a lower risk level than non-domestic PEPs, and enhanced due diligence measures are less extensive for domestic PEPs if no additional risk factors are present. The regulations must follow negative procedure and be laid before Parliament within 12 months.

sections.summary: This section mandates the FCA to review its guidance on politically exposed persons (PEPs) to assess compliance and determine if revisions are needed. The FCA must ensure publication methods are effective and avoid disclosing information against public interest.

sections.summary: This section mandates that any expenditure incurred by the Treasury for purposes related to the Financial Services and Markets Act 2023 must be funded by money provided by Parliament. requirements.text: Treasury must use parliamentary appropriations for expenditures related to this Act

sections.source_quote: There is to be paid out of money provided by Parliament any expenditure incurred by the Treasury for any purpose in connection with this Act.

It identifies specific SIs that modify financial regulations, including those related to market infrastructure, credit institutions, insurance, payment systems, and securities. These instruments are part of the UK's transition to post-EU regulatory framework after Brexit. This section lists specific EU directives relevant to financial services. The directives cover payment systems, credit institution reorganisation, securities listing, financial collateral, consumer financial marketing, and supervision of financial conglomerates. These directives are referenced for their applicability within the UK financial regulatory framework.

sections.title: Amendments to financial promotion and permission procedures

sections.heading: Short title (cont.)
sections.title: Recognition of DSA service providers for payment systems
sections.summary: The Treasury may issue recognition orders specifying DSA service providers as recognised entities for payment systems. These orders must detail services provided and cannot include the Bank of England. Recognition requires the Treasury to assess that service deficiencies or disruptions would not threaten financial system stability or cause serious business consequences across the UK. The Treasury must consider service value, nature, alternatives, and relationships with payment system operators and other DSA providers.
sections.dtia_summary: Recognition of DSA service providers requires assessment of financial stability risks and business impacts before cross-border payment system operations can proceed.

sections.heading: Short title (cont.)
sections.title: Digital Settlement Asset Definitions and Recognition
sections.summary: This section amends definitions related to digital settlement assets and recognised DSA service providers. It specifies that a digital settlement asset has the meaning given by section 41(2A) and a digital settlement asset exchange provider has the meaning given by section 182(5B) of the Banking Act 2009. It also defines a recognised DSA service provider as a DSA service provider with a recognition order under section 184A of the Banking Act 2009 in force. These definitions are critical for understanding the regulatory framework around digital payment systems.

sections.heading: Short title (cont.)
sections.title: Payment Systems Regulator Accountability and Consultation
sections.summary: This section establishes the Payment Systems Regulator's duty to review generally applicable requirements, prepare and publish statements of policy on reviews, and consult with statutory panels. It mandates that the Regulator must keep requirements under review, publish policy statements, and ensure consultations with relevant panels including the FCA Cost Benefit Analysis Panel. The Treasury may direct reviews of requirements if they have been in force for at least 12 months and the Treasury considers it in the public interest.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires the Payment Systems Regulator to review and publish policy on generally applicable requirements, ensuring transparency and accountability in cross-border payment system regulations.

requirements.text: The Payment Systems Regulator must keep under review generally any generally applicable requirements
The Payment Systems Regulator must prepare and publish a statement of its policy with respect to its review of requirements
The Payment Systems Regulator must consult the FCA Cost Benefit Analysis Panel on cost benefit analyses and policy statements
The Treasury may direct the Payment Systems Regulator to review a generally applicable requirement if it has been in force for at least 12 months and the Treasury considers it in the public interest

sections.source_quote: The Payment Systems Regulator must keep under review generally any generally applicable requirements.

sections.heading: Short title (cont.)
sections.title: Definition of international trade obligation for financial services
sections.summary: This section defines 'international trade obligation' as an obligation of the UK relating to financial services or markets under a free trade agreement as defined by section 5(1) of the Trade Act 2021 or the World Trade Organisation agreement signed at Marrakesh on 15 April 1994. The definition specifies the scope of obligations that may impact financial services regulation.
sections.dtia_summary: Defines the scope of international trade obligations that could affect financial services regulation, relevant for assessing cross-border data transfers under trade agreements.

requirements.text: international trade obligation means an obligation of the United Kingdom that relates to financial services or markets under a free trade agreement as defined by section 5(1) of the Trade Act 2

sections.source_quote: international trade obligation means an obligation of the United Kingdom that relates to financial services or markets under— (a) a free trade agreement, as defined by section 5(1) of the Trade Act 2021, or (b) the agreement establishing the World Trade Organisation signed at Marrakesh on 15 April 1994.

sections.heading: Short title (cont.)
sections.title: Cash access services and policy statement requirements
sections.summary: This section establishes requirements for cash access services, including definitions of cash, cash access services, and relevant current accounts. It mandates the Treasury to prepare and publish a cash access policy statement that addresses cash deposit and withdrawal services, current account types, and regional distribution of services. The policy statement must be prepared with consultation from the FCA and must consider reports on cash access services.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires Treasury to publish cash access policy statements that define cash services and regional access, relevant for assessing cross-border cash movement and service provision impacts.

requirements.text: The Treasury must prepare a cash access policy statement
A cash access policy statement is a statement of the policies of His Majesty's Government concerning cash access services in the United Kingdom, or a part of the United Kingdom
The Treasury must publish a cash access policy statement in such manner as they consider appropriate
The Treasury must keep the cash access policy statement under review
The Treasury may prepare a revised statement (and subsections (5) and (6) apply in relation to any revised statement)

sections.source_quote: The Treasury must prepare a cash access policy statement. A "cash access policy statement" is a statement of the policies of His Majesty's Government concerning cash access services in the United Kingdom, or a part of the United Kingdom.

sections.heading: Short title (cont.)
sections.title: FCA direction implementation and review procedures
sections.summary: This section outlines the requirements for FCA notices when implementing directions under section 131W. It mandates that notices must detail the direction, FCA's reasons for the direction and its determination of effective date, inform the designated person of their right to make representations and refer to the Tribunal, and require written notice if the FCA decides to give, revoke, or modify the direction after considering representations. The FCA may extend the representation period and must specify Tribunal referral procedures.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes procedural safeguards for FCA directions, requiring clear communication of direction details, reasons, effective dates, and review mechanisms. It impacts DTIAs by mandating transparency in cross-border regulatory decisions and ensuring third-party review rights.

requirements.text: Notices must detail the direction

sections.source_quote: A notice under subsection (6) must inform the notified person of the person's right to refer the matter to the Tribunal.

sections.heading: Short title (cont.)
sections.title: Consultation with regulators before enforcement actions
sections.summary: This section requires the Bank of England to consult the Financial Conduct Authority (FCA) before taking enforcement action against FCA-regulated persons. It mandates consultation with the Prudential Regulation Authority (PRA) for PRA-regulated activities and the Payment Systems Regulator for participants in regulated payment systems. These consultations must occur prior to any enforcement actions under the Part.
sections.dtia_summary: Regulators must be consulted before enforcement actions on cross-border financial entities, impacting data transfer assessments for financial institutions operating in multiple jurisdictions.

requirements.text: Consult FCA before taking enforcement action against FCA-regulated persons
Consult PRA before taking enforcement action against PRA-regulated activity participants
Consult Payment Systems Regulator before taking enforcement action against regulated payment system participants

sections.heading: Short title (cont.)
sections.title: Scotland-specific interpretation of injunctions
sections.summary: This section provides Scotland-specific interpretations for injunctions under Part 206Y. It clarifies that references to orders restraining actions in Scotland are treated as interdicts, orders requiring steps are specific performance orders under the Court of Session Act 1988, and references to remedying failures include mitigating effects while references to dealing with assets include asset disposal.
sections.dtia_summary: Clarifies jurisdictional interpretations for cross-border enforcement actions in Scotland, relevant for data transfer impact assessments involving Scottish entities.

requirements.text: References to orders restraining anything in Scotland are read as interdicts
References to orders requiring steps in Scotland are read as specific performance orders under section 45 of the Court of Session Act 1988
References to remedying a failure include mitigating its effect

sections.source_quote: (a) references to an order restraining anything are, in Scotland, to be read as references to an interdict prohibiting that thing; (b) references to an order requiring steps to be taken are, in Scotland, to be read as references to an order for specific performance under section 45 of the Court of Session Act 1988; (c) references to remedying a failure include mitigating its effect; (d) references to dealing with assets include disposing of them.

sections.heading: Short title (cont.)
sections.title: Amendments to Financial Services Act 2012 for wholesale cash distribution
sections.summary: This section amends Section 85 of the Financial Services Act 2012 to include 'wholesale cash distribution' under the relevant functions for complaints schemes. It also modifies Section 110 to add 'wholesale cash distribution' to the list of sections for penalties paid to the Treasury. The amendments update the Financial Services Act 2012 to incorporate wholesale cash distribution into the regulatory framework.

sections.heading: Short title (cont.)
sections.title: Prohibition orders for relevant recognised bodies
sections.summary: This section establishes Part 18 prohibition orders that allow regulators to prohibit individuals from performing specific functions related to relevant recognised bodies if they are not fit and proper persons. It outlines procedures for making, varying, and withdrawing these orders, including consultation requirements and notification duties to affected individuals. The framework ensures that prohibited individuals cannot perform functions that could pose risks to financial stability.
sections.dtia_summary: This section creates a mechanism for regulators to prohibit individuals from performing functions related to relevant recognised bodies, which impacts data transfer and processing by requiring due diligence on personnel involved in financial activities.

sections.heading: Short title (cont.)
sections.title: Definition of interested parties for approval applications
sections.summary: This section defines who constitutes 'interested parties' for applications seeking approval to perform designated senior management functions. The interested parties include the applicant, the person for whom the application is made, and the entity retaining that person to perform the function, unless the applicant is that entity. This definition applies to specific regulatory approval processes under section 309I.
sections.dtia_summary: Defines key parties involved in regulatory approval processes, which is relevant for DTIAs when assessing cross-border data transfers involving approved entities.

sections.heading: Short title (cont.)
sections.title: Definition of interested parties for approval withdrawal
sections.summary: This section defines who constitutes 'interested parties' for approvals related to designated senior management functions. The interested parties include the relevant recognised body that applied for the approval, the person for whom the approval was given, and the entity retaining that person's services, unless the relevant recognised body is that entity. This definition applies to withdrawal procedures under section 309G.
sections.dtia_summary: Defines key parties involved in regulatory approval processes, which is relevant for DTIAs when assessing cross-border data transfers involving approved entities.

sections.heading: Short title (cont.)
sections.title: Disciplinary action procedures for regulated persons
sections.summary: The appropriate regulator must issue warning notices to persons before taking disciplinary action under section 309Z2, including specifying the penalty amount, suspension period, or condition duration. The regulator must also provide decision notices with the same details after taking action. Persons may refer disputes to the Tribunal, and the regulator must publish statements of misconduct to relevant parties and the Treasury.
sections.dtia_summary: Regulators must notify affected parties before imposing disciplinary sanctions, including penalties, suspensions, or conditions on regulated persons. This affects DTIA by requiring clear communication channels and documentation for cross-border disciplinary actions involving financial institutions.

sections.heading: Short title (cont.)
sections.title: Public disclosure of disciplinary actions
sections.summary: The appropriate regulator must publish disciplinary statements to the public, including details of representations made to the regulator and any differences between draft and final statements. The regulator must also send copies to the Treasury and may charge reasonable fees for providing copies to individuals.
sections.dtia_summary: Regulators must publicly disclose disciplinary actions, which impacts DTIA by requiring transparency in disciplinary processes for cross-border financial entities and ensuring compliance with international reporting standards.

sections.heading: Short title (cont.)
sections.title: Definition of Part 18 approved person
sections.summary: This section defines a 'Part 18 approved person' as a person approved by the FCA under section 309G. The definition specifies that approval relates to the relevant recognised body on whose application approval was granted. The definition clarifies the scope of individuals subject to Part 18 oversight.
sections.dtia_summary: This definition is critical for DTIAs as it establishes who qualifies as a 'Part 18 approved person' under the regulatory framework, directly impacting cross-border data transfers involving approved individuals.

requirements.text: Part 18 approved person means a person in relation to whom the FCA has given its approval under section 309G

sections.source_quote: Part 18 approved person means a person in relation to whom the FCA has given its approval under section 309G.

sections.heading: Short title (cont.)
sections.title: Special resolution regime for central counterparties
sections.summary: This section outlines the special resolution regime for central counterparties (CCPs) to address financial difficulties. It identifies eight stabilisation options, including transferring to a private sector purchaser or bridge CCP, and specifies the roles of the Bank, Treasury, PRA, and FCA in the regime. The regime aims to ensure financial stability during CCP crises.
sections.dtia_summary: This section matters for DTIAs as it establishes the regulatory framework for CCPs, which may involve cross-border data transfers and requires specific safeguards for financial stability during resolution events.

requirements.text: The purpose of the special resolution regime for CCPs is to address financial difficulties

The eight stabilisation options include transfer to a private sector purchaser, bridge CCP, ownership transfer, terminating clearing member contracts, cash calls, reducing variation margin payments, writing down liabilities, and taking control of the CCP
The Bank, Treasury, PRA, and FCA have roles in the operation of the special resolution regime

sections.source_quote: The purpose of the special resolution regime for CCPs (see Part 5 of this Schedule) is to address the situation where all or part of the business of a CCP has encountered, or is likely to encounter, financial difficulties.

sections.heading: Short title (cont.)
sections.title: Removal of directors and senior managers with temporary manager appointment
sections.summary: The Bank may require a central counterparty (CCP) to remove directors or senior managers if specific financial or conduct conditions are met. The Bank may also require the CCP to replace removed directors/senior managers and take steps to effect the replacement, including calling shareholder meetings. The Bank may appoint a temporary manager to replace directors or work with them, with specific functions including assessing financial position and preserving the CCP's financial health. Temporary managers must be appointed with clear functions, have no conflicts of interest, and may be reappointed if conditions continue.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes conditions for removing directors/senior managers and appointing temporary managers during financial distress, requiring specific safeguards like temporary manager qualifications, reporting obligations, and clear procedures for implementation to ensure orderly resolution of CCP failures.

requirements.text: The Bank may require a CCP to remove any director or senior manager if conditions in paragraph 7(1) and (2) are met
The Bank may require a CCP to replace removed directors/senior managers and take steps to effect the replacement

A temporary manager must have qualifications, ability, and knowledge to carry out functions

A temporary manager may not be appointed for more than one year but may be reappointed if conditions continue

The temporary manager must specify functions, effective date, appointment period, and resignation/replacement procedures
The temporary manager may require consultation with the Bank before exercising specified functions
The temporary manager must report financial position and actions to the Bank

sections.source_quote: If the Bank is satisfied that the conditions in paragraph 7(1) and (2) are met in relation to a CCP, the Bank may require the CCP to remove—(a) any person who is a director of the CCP; (b) any person who is a senior manager of the CCP.

sections.heading: Short title (cont.)
sections.title: Financial assistance cases for CCP stabilisation powers
sections.summary: The Bank must consider minimising effects on other group members and financial stability outside the UK when exercising stabilisation powers for CCPs in a group. Financial assistance cases require Treasury notification and Bank approval that stabilisation is necessary for public interest and appropriate for protection. The Bank must satisfy conditions related to Treasury recommendations and assessment of stabilisation effectiveness before exercising powers.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires specific conditions for stabilisation powers in financial assistance cases, including Treasury recommendations and Bank assessment of public interest protection. Affects cross-border data transfers through CCPs during financial crises.

sections.source_quote: Where the Bank is considering the exercise of a stabilisation power in respect of a CCP which is a member of a group, the Bank must have regard to—(a) the need to minimise the effect of the exercise of the power on other undertakings in the same group, and (b) the potential effect of the exercise of the power on the financial stability of countries other than the United Kingdom (particularly those countries in which any member of that group is operating).

sections.heading: Short title (cont.)
sections.title: Financial assistance cases for CCP stabilisation powers
sections.summary: The Bank must ensure valuations of CCP assets and liabilities before exercising stabilisation powers. Valuations must inform decisions on stabilisation options, liability adjustments, and asset transfers. The Bank must appoint independent valuers and comply with specific valuation requirements including balance sheets, financial reports, and creditor analyses.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires detailed valuation procedures for CCPs before stabilisation powers, including balance sheets, financial reports, and creditor analyses. Critical for assessing cross-border financial risks during CCP failures.

requirements.text: Bank must ensure assets and liabilities of CCP are valued before exercising stabilisation power
Valuation must inform decisions on stabilisation options, liability adjustments, and asset transfers

sections.source_quote: Before the Bank exercises a stabilisation power in respect of a CCP, it must ensure that the assets and liabilities of the CCP are valued.

sections.heading: Short title (cont.)
sections.title: Write-down instruments for CCPs
sections.summary: The Bank must create write-down instruments to cancel, modify, or restructure unsecured liabilities of a Central Counterparty (CCP) to recover losses not arising from clearing member defaults. These instruments can affect securities, including shares, debentures, and other debt instruments. The Bank must ensure provisions comply with insolvency treatment principles to maintain creditor equality during liquidation.
sections.dtia_summary: Write-down instruments allow the Bank to restructure CCP liabilities without affecting critical creditors like employees, small businesses, or critical service providers. This impacts cross-border data transfers involving CCPs by requiring specific safeguards for liability restructuring.

requirements.text: The Bank must make write-down instruments to cancel, modify, or restructure unsecured liabilities of a CCP

Write-down instruments may modify or change the form of unsecured liabilities owed by the CCP
Write-down instruments may treat contracts as if a specified right has been exercised
Write-down instruments may not affect liabilities to employees, critical service providers, HMRC debts, or small enterprises
Write-down instruments must comply with insolvency treatment principles to ensure equal creditor treatment

sections.source_quote: A write-down instrument is an instrument that makes any of the following provision (or any combination of the following)—(a) provision cancelling an unsecured liability owed by the CCP; (b) provision modifying or changing the form of an unsecured liability owed by the CCP; (c) provision that a contract under which the CCP has an unsecured liability is to have effect as if a specified right had been exercised under it; (d) provision under paragraph 35(1).

sections.heading: Short title (cont.)
sections.title: Reporting on write-down instruments
sections.summary: The Bank must report to the Chancellor of the Exchequer the reasons for any provisions in write-down instruments, including why they depart from insolvency treatment principles. Reports must be made promptly and laid before Parliament. The Bank must ensure provisions align with insolvency treatment principles to maintain equal creditor treatment during liquidation.
sections.dtia_summary: This section requires transparency in how CCP liabilities are restructured, which affects data transfer impact assessments by mandating clear reporting on cross-border financial restructuring activities.

requirements.text: The Bank must report to the Chancellor of the Exchequer the reasons for provisions in write-down instruments

sections.heading: Short title (cont.)
sections.title: Share transfer instruments: conditions for effect and registration
sections.summary: Share transfer instruments may permit or require execution, issue, or delivery of instruments. Transfers may take effect regardless of production, delivery, or registration status. Instruments may entitle transferees to registration or require registration effects. These instruments may also include incidental provisions and modify or annul instrument effects.
sections.dtia_summary: These provisions define conditions under which share transfers take effect without registration, relevant for cross-border data transfers involving central counterparties.

requirements.text: A share transfer instrument may permit or require the execution, issue or delivery of an instrument.

A share transfer instrument may provide for the effect of an instrument executed, issued or delivered, in accordance with the instrument.
A share transfer instrument may modify or annul the effect of an instrument.
A share transfer instrument may entitle a transferee to be registered in respect of transferred securities.
A share transfer instrument may require a person to effect registration.
A share transfer instrument may include incidental, consequential or transitional provision.
A share transfer instrument may make different provision for different purposes, cases or circumstances.

sections.source_quote: A share transfer instrument may permit or require the execution, issue or delivery of an instrument.

sections.heading: Short title (cont.)
sections.title: Property transfer effectiveness and foreign law compliance
sections.summary: The transferor and transferee must ensure the transfer is effective under foreign law. Until effective, the transferor must hold property for the transferee's benefit or discharge liabilities on their behalf. If foreign law prevents effectiveness, the transferor must cease obligations and the transfer provisions become void. The Bank must notify parties of any determinations and the transferor covers transferee costs. Obligations are enforceable as contracts between parties. 'Foreign law' refers to law outside the UK.
sections.dtia_summary: This section mandates that cross-border property transfers must comply with foreign law, requiring specific actions to ensure effectiveness. If foreign law prevents effectiveness, the transfer becomes void. The Bank must notify parties and the transferor bears costs. Critical for DTIA as it defines when transfers are legally effective across borders.
