Transferor must comply with Bank's directions regarding obligations under sub-paragraphs (3) and (4)
Bank may disapply sub-paragraphs (3) and (4) to a specified extent through directions
Obligations imposed by Bank directions are enforceable as contracts between transferor and Bank
Foreign law means law of a country or territory outside the United Kingdom

sections.source_quote: The transferor and the transferee must each take any necessary steps to ensure that the transfer is effective as a matter of foreign law (if it is not wholly effective by virtue of the property transfer instrument).

sections.heading: Short title (cont.)
sections.title: Partial property transfers for protected arrangements
sections.summary: This section imposes conditions on partial property transfers involving protected arrangements, requiring specified provisions to be included, and allowing for voidance or consequences if transfers contravene regulations. These obligations apply to property transfer instruments and entities creating such transfers.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires specific safeguards for partial property transfers involving protected arrangements across borders to prevent unintended consequences.

requirements.text: Restrict partial property transfers in cases involving protected arrangements
Impose conditions on partial property transfers involving protected arrangements
Require partial property transfers to include specified provision for protected arrangements
Make partial property transfers void or voidable if contravening regulations

sections.source_quote: restrict the making of partial property transfers in cases that involve, or where they might affect, protected arrangements

sections.heading: Short title (cont.)
sections.title: Resolution instruments: publication and parliamentary notification
sections.summary: This section mandates that the Bank publish resolution instruments to affected parties and lay copies before Parliament after making them, ensuring transparency and accountability in financial resolution processes.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires publication of resolution instruments to ensure transparency and parliamentary oversight for cross-border financial resolutions.

requirements.text: Publish resolution instruments to persons likely to be affected
Lay copies of resolution instruments before Parliament

sections.source_quote: As soon as is reasonably practicable after making a resolution instrument the Bank must publish a copy—

sections.heading: Short title (cont.)
sections.title: Independent valuers' procedural and liability provisions
sections.summary: This section outlines specific provisions for independent valuers, including their authority to confer functions, principles for determining compensation, procedures for valuations, liability frameworks, and appeal mechanisms. These provisions apply to independent valuers engaged in financial services activities under the Financial Services and Markets Act 2023.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes procedural and liability requirements for independent valuers, which may impact data transfer assessments when valuers handle financial data across borders.

requirements.text: Provision conferring functions on independent valuers (including discretion)
Provision specifying principles for independent valuers to determine compensation
Provision about procedure for independent valuers
Provision about liability of independent valuers
Provision about appeals against decisions by independent valuers (including conferring jurisdiction on courts or tribunals)

sections.source_quote: provision conferring functions on independent valuers (including conferring a discretion), provision specifying principles to be applied by independent valuers to determine the amount of compensation, provision about the procedure to be followed by independent valuers, provision about the liability of independent valuers, and provision about appeals against decisions by independent valuers (including conferring jurisdiction on a court or tribunal).

sections.heading: Short title (cont.)
sections.title: CCP resolution: termination rights during suspension
sections.summary: This section defines conditions under which a CCP under resolution may terminate contracts with subsidiaries. Termination rights are preserved if the subsidiary's obligations are guaranteed by the CCP, termination is triggered by the CCP's insolvency, and property transfers occur to a single transferee. The Bank must consider suspension impacts on contractual obligations. Termination rights can be exercised before suspension expiry if the Bank notifies that rights are not transferable or subject to instruments.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes safeguards for cross-border data transfers involving CCPs during financial distress by defining termination rights, property transfer requirements, and notification obligations that affect data transfer continuity.

requirements.text: A contract is a qualifying contract if one party is a CCP under resolution and obligations continue
A contract is a qualifying contract if one party is a subsidiary of a CCP under resolution and termination conditions are met
Subsidiary obligations must be guaranteed or supported by the CCP under resolution
Termination rights must be triggered by insolvency or financial condition of CCP under resolution
All assets and liabilities must transfer to a single transferee or Bank provides adequate protection
Bank must consider suspension impact on contractual obligations
Termination rights may be exercised before suspension expiry if Bank notifies rights are not transferable
Termination rights may be exercised before suspension expiry if Bank notifies rights are not subject to instruments

sections.source_quote: A contract is a "qualifying contract" for the purpose of this paragraph if— (a) one of the parties to the contract is a CCP in respect of which the Bank is exercising a stabilisation power (a "CCP under resolution") and all the obligations under the contract to make a payment, make delivery or provide collateral continue to be performed, or (b) one of the parties to the contract is a subsidiary of a CCP under resolution and the condition in sub-paragraph (3) is met.

sections.heading: Short title (cont.)
sections.title: CCP resolution: court stay for stabilisation powers
sections.summary: This section permits the Bank to seek court stays or suspensions of legal proceedings when exercising stabilisation powers over CCPs. The Bank may apply to UK courts for stays if it reasonably considers such measures are necessary for effective stabilisation options or powers. This ensures legal proceedings do not interfere with critical financial resolution mechanisms.
sections.dtia_summary: This section provides a mechanism for court stays during CCP resolutions, ensuring data transfer processes can continue without legal interference when stabilisation powers are exercised.

requirements.text: Bank may apply to UK courts for stay or sist of proceedings
Bank must reasonably consider stay necessary for effective application of stabilisation options

sections.source_quote: The Bank may apply to that court for a stay or sist of proceedings where the Bank reasonably considers that a stay or sist of those proceedings is necessary for an effective application of the stabilisation options or the stabilisation powers.

sections.heading: Short title (cont.)
sections.title: Bank consultation requirements for CCP group companies
sections.summary: The Bank must consult the Treasury and the FCA before determining if Condition 2 or 3 applies to a CCP group company. The Bank must also consider minimizing impacts on other group companies when exercising stabilisation powers. A financial assistance case involves the Treasury providing financial assistance to resolve serious threats to UK financial stability.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires Bank consultation with regulators before determining cross-border CCP group company compliance; affects data transfer assessments involving financial stability interventions.

requirements.text: Bank must consult Treasury and FCA before determining Condition 2 or 3 for CCP group companies
Bank must minimize impact on other group companies when exercising stabilisation powers
Financial assistance case means Treasury providing financial assistance to resolve serious threats to UK financial stability

sections.source_quote: Before determining whether Condition 2 or 3 (as appropriate) is met, the Bank must consult—(a) the Treasury, (b) the FCA.

sections.heading: Short title (cont.)
sections.title: Investigator appointment and reporting requirements
sections.summary: The Bank may appoint investigators to conduct investigations into CCPs or related entities. Investigators must report findings to the Bank and may require information or documents from individuals connected to the subject of investigation. The Bank must provide written notice of appointments and specify reasons for appointments.
sections.dtia_summary: Defines investigator powers for data transfer impact assessments; requires reporting mechanisms for cross-border financial investigations.

requirements.text: Bank may appoint investigators to conduct investigations into CCPs or related entities
Investigators must report findings to the Bank
Bank must provide written notice of investigator appointments to the person under investigation
Notice must specify provisions under which investigator was appointed and reason for appointment

sections.source_quote: The Bank must give written notice of the appointment of an investigator to the person who is the subject of the investigation.

sections.heading: Short title (cont.)
sections.title: Entry of premises under warrant for financial investigations
sections.summary: A justice of the peace may issue a warrant for entry into premises if satisfied on oath that specific conditions are met, including failures to comply with information requirements, presence of relevant documents or information, or ongoing offences. The warrant authorises constables to enter premises, search, take possession of documents, make copies, require explanations, and use reasonable force. The powers apply in England and Wales, Northern Ireland, and Scotland with specific adaptations.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes conditions for warrant-based entry into premises during financial investigations, impacting data transfer and access across jurisdictions. It requires specific compliance with information requirements before authorising searches, affecting data collection and retention procedures.

requirements.text: A justice of the peace may issue a warrant if satisfied on oath that there are reasonable grounds for believing the first, second or third set of conditions is satisfied
The first set of conditions requires a person failing to comply with an information requirement and documents or information on the premises
The second set of conditions requires premises of a CCP or group member, relevant documents or information, and risk of non-compliance or destruction
The third set of conditions requires an ongoing offence, relevant documents or information, and risk of non-compliance or destruction
A warrant authorises constables to enter premises and search for documents of the relevant kind
A warrant authorises constables to take possession of documents and take steps to preserve them
A warrant authorises constables to make copies or extracts of relevant documents
A warrant authorises constables to require explanations of documents or information
A warrant authorises constables to use reasonable force

sections.source_quote: A justice of the peace may issue a warrant under this paragraph if satisfied on information on oath given by or on behalf of the Secretary of State, the Bank or an investigator that there are reasonable grounds for believing that the first, second or third set of conditions is satisfied.

sections.heading: Short title (cont.)
sections.title: Retention and disposal of seized financial documents
sections.summary: Seized documents may be retained for as long as necessary in the circumstances. Owners may apply to courts for delivery of seized documents, and courts may order delivery if ownership cannot be ascertained. Rights to legal proceedings for recovery of seized documents are limited to six months from the order date.
sections.dtia_summary: This section governs the retention period and legal procedures for seized financial documents, impacting data storage duration and disposal mechanisms in cross-border financial investigations.

requirements.text: Seized documents may be retained for as long as necessary in the circumstances
A person claiming ownership may apply to a magistrates' court or sheriff for delivery of seized documents
Courts may make orders for delivery if ownership cannot be ascertained
Orders for delivery do not affect rights to legal proceedings for recovery of seized documents
Rights to legal proceedings for recovery of seized documents may only be exercised within six months of the order

sections.source_quote: Any document of which possession is taken under paragraph 130 may be retained for so long as it is necessary to retain it (rather than copies of it) in the circumstances.

sections.heading: Short title (cont.)
sections.title: Publication restrictions for Bank notices
sections.summary: This section prohibits the publication of warning or decision notices by the Bank or recipients without prior Bank publication. Recipients may not publish notice details unless the Bank has published them. The Bank may publish information about matters to which notices relate after consulting recipients. Publication of information must be anonymous if it jeopardises financial stability, ongoing investigations, or causes disproportionate damage. Published information must remain online for at least five years unless data protection laws require otherwise.
sections.dtia_summary: Publication restrictions for Bank notices require coordination with the Bank before external disclosure. Anonymous publication may be necessary to protect financial stability or ongoing investigations. Information must remain online for five years unless data protection laws require otherwise.

requirements.text: Neither the Bank nor a recipient may publish a warning notice
A recipient may not publish notice details unless the Bank has published them
The Bank may publish information about matters to which notices relate after consulting recipients
Published information must remain online for at least five years unless data protection laws require otherwise
Published information must be anonymous if it jeopardises financial stability or ongoing investigations
Published information must be anonymous if it causes disproportionate damage to persons involved

sections.source_quote: 357 (1) In the case of a warning notice under paragraph 138— (a) neither the Bank nor a person to whom it is given or copied may publish the notice, (b) a person to whom the notice is given or copied may not publish any details concerning the notice unless the Bank has published those details, and (c) after consulting the persons to whom the notice is given or copied, the Bank may publish such information about the matter to which the notice relates as it considers appropriate.

sections.heading: Short title (cont.)
sections.title: Third-country resolution actions and instruments
sections.summary: This section governs the Bank's recognition of third-country resolution actions for central counterparties. The Bank must issue instruments recognizing, refusing to recognize, or partially recognizing third-country resolution actions. Recognition may be refused if it adversely affects financial stability, causes unfair treatment of UK creditors, has fiscal implications, or violates human rights. Recognition produces legal effects in the UK equivalent to domestic resolution actions.
sections.dtia_summary: Third-country resolution actions require Bank recognition with specific conditions to avoid financial instability or unfair treatment. Recognition creates binding legal effects in the UK equivalent to domestic resolution actions.

requirements.text: The Bank must make an instrument recognizing, refusing to recognize, or partially recognizing third-country resolution actions
Recognition may be refused if it adversely affects financial stability
Recognition may be refused if it causes unfair treatment of UK creditors
Recognition may be refused if it has fiscal implications for the UK
Recognition may be refused if it violates human rights
Recognition produces legal effects in the UK equivalent to domestic resolution actions

sections.source_quote: 145 (1) This paragraph applies where the Bank is notified of third-country resolution action in respect of a third-country central counterparty. (2) The Bank must make an instrument which— (a) recognises the action, (b) refuses to recognise the action, or (c) recognises part of the action and refuses to recognise the remainder.

sections.heading: Short title (cont.)
sections.title: Definitions for financial assistance and central counterparties
sections.summary: This section defines key terms used in the context of financial assistance and central counterparties (CCPs). It specifies meanings for terms like 'financial assistance', 'group', 'interoperable CCP', 'PRA-authorised person', and 'recognised central counterparty'. These definitions are critical for understanding the scope and application of subsequent provisions related to financial assistance mechanisms and CCP recognition.
sections.dtia_summary: Defines terms essential for understanding financial assistance mechanisms and CCP recognition, which are relevant for cross-border data transfers involving financial institutions and CCPs.

requirements.text: financial assistance has the meaning given by paragraph 152
group has the meaning given by section 474 of the Companies Act 2006
interoperable CCP means a CCP with which an interoperability arrangement (within the meaning of Article 2 of EMIR) has been established
PRA-authorised person has the meaning given by section 2B(5) of FSMA 2000
recognition requirements means the requirements resulting from section 286 of FSMA 2000
resolution instrument has the meaning given by paragraph 79
securities has the meaning given by paragraph 40
service contract has the meaning given by section 227 of the Companies Act 2006
senior manager, in relation to a CCP, means a person who exercises executive functions within that CCP and is responsible for day-to-day management
special resolution objectives means the objectives set out in paragraph 15
stabilisation instrument has the meaning given by paragraph 84
stabilisation options means the options described in paragraph 1(3)
stabilisation powers means the powers described in paragraph 1(4)
subsidiary means a subsidiary undertaking within the meaning given by section 1162 of the Companies Act 2006
tear-up instrument has the meaning given by paragraph 31
third-country instrument has the meaning given by paragraph 145
third-country resolution action has the meaning given by paragraph 145
transfer date— in relation to a share transfer instrument, means the date on or at which a share transfer instrument takes effect
UK financial system has the meaning given by section 1I of FSMA 2000
variation instrument has the meaning given by paragraph 33
write-down order has the meaning given by paragraph 108

sections.heading: Short title (cont.)
sections.title: Restrictions on confidential information disclosure
sections.summary: This section modifies existing disclosure rules to include the Bank of England and Financial Services and Markets Act 2023 as entities that may disclose confidential information. Disclosure is permitted to specific authorities but must not contravene data protection laws.
sections.dtia_summary: Confidential information disclosure is restricted to specific authorities and must comply with data protection laws to avoid breaches.

sections.heading: Short title (cont.)
sections.title: Court orders for write-down orders in insolvency cases
sections.summary: The court may order a write-down order for insurers when certain conditions are met, including making the order in the terms sought or as the court deems appropriate. The court can also dismiss applications, adjourn hearings, or make other orders. This section applies to insurers seeking write-down orders under specific circumstances.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes court oversight for write-down orders in insolvency cases, requiring specific orders for insurers to manage liabilities during financial distress. It impacts data transfer by regulating how insurers handle liabilities across borders.

requirements.text: If satisfied of matters in section 377A(2), make a write-down order in terms sought or as court deems appropriate
Dismiss the application
Adjourn the hearing conditionally or unconditionally
Make any other order the court thinks appropriate

sections.source_quote: if, on hearing the application, it is satisfied of the matters in section 377A(2), make a write-down order in the terms sought, or in such other terms as the court thinks appropriate;

sections.heading: Short title (cont.)
sections.title: Manager of write-down orders for insurers
sections.summary: The court may appoint one or more eligible persons as managers for write-down orders to oversee insurer affairs. Managers must monitor the insurer's affairs to ensure the write-down order remains beneficial for policyholders and creditors. They can recommend actions to directors and provide information to regulators.
sections.dtia_summary: This section defines the role of managers in write-down orders, ensuring oversight of insurer activities during financial distress. It affects data transfer by regulating how insurers handle liabilities across borders.

requirements.text: Court may appoint one or more eligible persons as manager of write-down order
Manager must monitor insurer's affairs to form view on whether write-down order remains beneficial for policyholders and creditors
Manager may make recommendations to insurer's directors
Manager may require relevant persons to provide information or assistance

sections.source_quote: The court may by order appoint one or more eligible persons to act as the manager of a write-down order

sections.heading: Short title (cont.)
sections.title: Policyholder surrender rights during insurer financial difficulties
sections.summary: This section limits policyholders' right to surrender insurance contracts when an insurer faces financial difficulties. The surrender value cannot exceed 5% of the contract's total surrender value per 12-month period. It defines relevant contracts, periods, and surrender limits while allowing court or relevant office-holder consent to exceed limits if hardship is proven. Policyholders and insurers are affected.
sections.dtia_summary: Restricts policyholder surrender rights during insurer financial distress to prevent excessive claims, requiring 5% surrender limits per period. This impacts data transfer assessments for insurers handling policyholder data across borders during insolvency events.

requirements.text: Total value of policyholder rights under relevant contract must not exceed 5% of total surrender value per 12-month period
Relevant contract excludes short-term policies with death/incapacity benefits only
Relevant contract excludes policies with no surrender value or single premium
Relevant contract excludes convertible or extendable policies
Relevant office-holder may give consent to exceed surrender limits if hardship is proven
Consent must be given by court, relevant office-holder, or insurer
Consent must be notified to PRA within reasonable time

sections.source_quote: Where this Schedule applies in relation to an insurer, the total value of a policyholder's rights under a relevant contract of insurance which the policyholder may surrender in a relevant period must not exceed the surrender limit for that period.

sections.heading: Short title (cont.)
sections.title: Court order conditions for financial difficulties in insurers
sections.summary: This section outlines the conditions under which a court may order an intervention for an insurer in financial difficulties. The court must be satisfied that either (i) not making the order would cause hardship to any person other than the insurer, (ii) making the order is reasonably likely to improve outcomes for policyholders and creditors when the insurer faces financial difficulties via a write-down order, or (iii) making the order promotes the purpose of administration when the insurer is in administration. The purpose of administration is defined as specific objectives under the 1986 Act or 1989 Order.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes conditions for court-ordered interventions during insurer financial distress, which may impact data transfer decisions if insurers are involved in cross-border financial operations or data handling.

requirements.text: The court may make an order under this paragraph only if satisfied that not making the order would be likely to cause hardship to any person (other than the insurer).

The court may make an order under this paragraph only if satisfied that where the insurer is in financial difficulties by virtue of paragraph 2(1)(a) (write-down order), making the order is reasonably likely to lead to a better outcome for the insurer's policyholders and other creditors (taken as a whole) than not making the order.

The court may make an order under this paragraph only if satisfied that where the insurer is in financial difficulties by virtue of paragraph 2(1)(b) (administration), making the order is reasonably likely to promote the purpose of administration.
The 'purpose of administration' means an objective specified in paragraph 3 or 3A of Schedule B1 to the 1986 Act where the insurer is in, or is awaiting, administration under Part 2 of the 1986 Act.
The 'purpose of administration' means an objective specified in paragraph 4 of Schedule B1 to the 1989 Order where the insurer is in, or is awaiting, administration under Part 3 of the 1989 Order.

sections.source_quote: The court may make an order under this paragraph only if satisfied that one or more of the following grounds is made out— (a) not making the order would be likely to cause hardship to any person (other than the insurer); (b) where the insurer is in financial difficulties by virtue of paragraph 2(1)(a) (write-down order), making the order is reasonably likely to lead to a better outcome for the insurer's policyholders and other creditors (taken as a whole) than not making the order; (c) where the insurer is in financial difficulties by virtue of paragraph 2(1)(b) (administration), making the order is reasonably likely to promote the purpose of administration.

sections.heading: Short title (cont.)
sections.title: Credit union loan provisions and financial activities
sections.summary: This section amends credit union regulations to define the optional financial activities credit unions may engage in, including conditional sale agreements, hire purchase agreements, and insurance distribution activities. It specifies that credit unions may carry out these activities with members or other credit unions, with certain restrictions on the maximum repayment period for loans to non-member credit unions (six months) and the conditions under which agreements are treated as secured.
sections.dtia_summary: This section defines financial activities credit unions can perform, including cross-credit union transfers and conditional sales, which may affect data transfer when credit unions handle financial data across borders.

requirements.text: Credit unions may enter into conditional sale agreements as sellers with members.
Credit unions may enter into hire purchase agreements as the person from whom goods are bailed or hired with members.
Credit unions may carry out insurance distribution activities for members.
The maximum repayment period for loans to non-member credit unions is six months.
Credit unions may only enter into agreements with corporate members if their rules allow it and the aggregate outstanding balances with corporate members do not exceed 10% of those with members.

sections.source_quote: If the objects of a credit union include the optional object specified in section 1(3ZZA) it may (subject to such terms, including as to security, as its rules may provide)— (a) enter into a conditional sale agreement, as the seller, with a member of the credit union, or (b) enter into a hire purchase agreement, as the person from whom goods are bailed or (in Scotland) hired, with a member of the credit union.
